summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Ex-tempore reasons – Application for Review – Where hearing of the parties’ competing applications for final property orders is listed in approximately two months – Where the wife seeks review of orders made by a Senior Judicial Registrar requiring the valuation of property for the purposes of final hearing – Where the husband opposes the wife’s Application for Review – Where the property the subject of the disputed valuation order was last valued in June of 2024 – Where it is necessary and appropriate that an updated valuation be conducted prior to trial – Application for Review dismissed

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

On 4 December 2025, Senior Judicial Registrar Sudholz made interim orders (“the December orders”) regarding valuation of the parties’ property interests. The December orders provide as follows:-
The parties shall do all acts and things to obtain an updated valuation of the property at [C Street], [Suburb B] (“the property”) and for the purpose of same:
(a) Imperial Valuations shall be the nominated valuer;
(b) the Husband shall meet the costs of the valuation at first instance;

(c) all communications to the valuer shall be copied to the other party (or their solicitor if represented);

(d) for the purpose of instructing the valuer:

within 24 hours, the solicitor for the Husband shall forward the proposed letter of instruction to the Wife;
within 7 days, the Wife shall email to the Husband and the Associate to Senior Judicial Registrar Sudholz [...] written submissions of no more than 5 pages in length setting out any additions or deletions to the proposed letter of instruction and why they are required; and
within 14 days, the Husband shall email to the Wife and the Associate to Senior Judicial Registrar Sudholz [...] written submissions of no more than 5 pages in length setting out opposition to the proposed additions or deletions to the proposed letter of instruction and the basis of that opposition.
Within 24 hours of compliance with paragraphs 1(c)(iii) and 2(d)(iii) herein, the Associate to Senior Judicial Registrar Sudholz shall forward to the parties the settled letters of instruction referred to in paragraphs 1 and 2 herein, and such letters shall be forwarded by the solicitor for the Husband (and copied to the Wife) to the respective valuer named on the letter therein.
On 22 December 2025, the wife, Ms Harish, filed an Application for Review seeking a review of those orders. The orders sought by her in that application are:-
That the orders identified, being Orders 2 and 3 of the December orders be set aside;
That there be no further valuation undertaken in respect of the property at [C Street], [Suburb B];
That the existing valuation evidence be deemed sufficient for determination of the property pool;
Alternatively, if valuations are to be ordered, that they proportionally be directed to the respondent’s unvalued assets including:-
An apartment situate at [E Apartment Complex];
[Super Fund 1];
[F Pty Ltd] trust assets; and
[G Discretionary Trust].
Whilst that was the position of the wife at the time she filed her Application for Review, for the purposes of today’s hearing, the wife appears to have resiled from at least part of her application. Indeed, she indicated to the Court during the course of her submissions that she does not oppose the valuation of the Suburb B property. Instead, she maintained that there needs to be “proportionality” as to the focus on the valuation of that asset as compared with other assets that, on her assertion, comprise the parties’ asset pool; it was unclear what relief she sought to address that complaint.
The Application for Review filed by the wife is supported by her affidavit filed 12 December 2025.
The Application for Review is opposed by the respondent husband, Mr Sigihild, who is the applicant in the substantive proceedings. In support of that position the husband relies upon the Outline of Case document filed on his behalf on 12 February 2026, and written submissions also filed that date. He has also filed a Response to Application in a Proceeding dated 30 January 2026 and an affidavit, also filed 30 January 2026. Reliance on parts of that affidavit is identified in the written submissions filed on his behalf.
By way of background, I note the following:-
The wife was born in 1969 and is aged 56 years. She discloses her occupation to be an educator. She currently resides in the property at C Street, Suburb B, which is the property the subject of the order for valuation under review. The wife submitted that the Suburb B property was an asset that she brought into the parties’ relationship. Ultimately, whether that is an asset introduced to the parties’ marriage by the wife will be a matter for determination by the trial judge.
The husband was born in 1966 and is aged 59 years. He is a finance professional.
The parties married in 2016 and separated in March 2023.
There are no children of the marriage.
The wife submitted that each party has two children from prior relationships; the wife’s children are aged 18 and 23 years.
The proceedings were commenced by the husband upon the filing of his Application for Final Orders in November 2023. The parties’ competing property applications are listed for final hearing, before Williams J, noted as a four-day matter, to commence on 27 April 2026.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

An Application for Review must be filed within 21 days after the decision or order is made. That this is so is confirmed by rule 14.05 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”). The wife’s Application for Review is within time.
Rule 14.07 of the Rules sets out the procedure for the hearing of an Application for Review. Such review of an exercise of power by a Senior Judicial Registrar is conducted by way of an original hearing, that is, it is a hearing of the original application, a hearing de novo.
Prior to the commencement of the hearing, I informed the wife, who is self-represented, of the manner in which the hearing would be conducted. I informed her that as applicant, she would have the opportunity to make submissions in support of her case; that she could refer to evidence she relies upon in support of her position and make submissions as to the relevant legal principles. I also informed her that the husband’s counsel would then have opportunity to make submissions in response and that if, during the course of those submissions, matters were raised that had not been addressed by her, she would have opportunity to make a brief reply to those submissions. The wife confirmed her understanding of that process.
At the commencement of her submissions, the wife expressed concern that she had been denied procedural fairness insofar as the written submissions relied upon by the husband were not filed and served until approximately 4 pm on the day prior to the hearing.
The submissions of the husband as to the substantive part of the wife’s application, that is the question of whether or not the valuation orders should stand, span approximately three and a half pages.
The wife confirmed that, whilst she had read those submissions, she required additional time to read and understand them. As a result, the matter was stood down for a period of approximately 25 minutes to enable the wife to review the husband’s submissions.

THE WIFE’S CASE

The wife contended that the husband had previously made application for valuation of the C Street property and that that application had been dismissed. As such, she contended that he should not be permitted to relitigate that issue. In support of that submission, the wife referred to the compliance and readiness hearing conducted before Judge Boymal on 14 October 2025. It was at that hearing that the proceedings were transferred to this Court. The wife submitted that at that hearing, the husband sought orders for valuation of the Suburb B property. The wife confirmed that Judge Boymal declined to entertain any application for valuation of property at that hearing and indicated that those matters ought be referred to trial. In light of that submission, I am satisfied that there has not been a previous determination by a judge of the question of whether or not there ought be orders for an updated valuation of the Suburb B property.
During her oral submissions, the wife resiled from her position that the valuation orders ought be discharged. The wife referred to the hearing before Senior Judicial Registrar Sudholz which occurred on 4 December 2025 and acknowledged that at that hearing she opposed orders for valuation of the Suburb B property. Notwithstanding her position then, the wife confirmed during her oral submissions before me that she did not oppose an updated valuation being prepared in relation to the Suburb B property.
The wife’s submissions appeared to be directed towards what she considers to be a “lack of proportionality” in the Court’s approach to valuation of the parties’ assets. The wife complains that there has been focus on valuation of the Suburb B property in circumstances where she has been agitating that assets within the husband’s possession or control have not been valued.
I am not in a position to make any assessment as to what actions or otherwise have been taken in relation to valuation of assets within the husband’s possession or control. Whilst the complaints raised by the wife as to valuation of other assets do not fall within the ambit of this application, I note that detailed orders were made at a case management hearing on 5 February 2026 by Williams J, she being the trial judge charged with the task of hearing the parties’ competing applications for final property orders on 27 April 2026. Those orders provide for the husband to make disclosure in relation to his interests including, but not limited to, his self‑managed superannuation fund, and the G Discretionary Trust. In addition to those orders, orders were also made for the valuation of a property at D Street, Suburb H held by the husband.
In light of those orders, it seems that matters are well in train in terms of identifying and valuing the parties’ assets.

CONCLUSION

The only issue that must be determined today is the question of whether or not the valuation orders in relation to the Suburb B property ought be maintained. That property was last valued by a single expert valuer on 26 June 2024, the single expert valuer at that time being Herron Todd White. During her oral submissions, the wife conceded that that was the last occasion on which a jointly appointed expert valued the property.
The wife was informed that the approach by the Court in determining competing applications under s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) “the Act”) requires an identification and valuation of each of the parties’ assets. She was also informed that the relevant time for valuation is as at the date of trial. The orders, as framed by Senior Judicial Registrar Sudholz, enable that process to be undertaken.
During her oral submissions, the wife did not raise any serious challenge to orders for valuation of the Suburb B property.
It was submitted on behalf of the husband that in circumstances where the hearing of the parties’ competing s 79 applications is listed in April 2026, the orders made by Senior Judicial Registrar Sudholz on 4 December 2025 were entirely appropriate to enable orderly trial preparation. Accordingly, he contended the wife’s application ought be dismissed.
During the course of her submissions, the wife raised concerns as to the reliability of the valuer who prepared the first valuation in 2024, being Herron Todd White. It was submitted on behalf of the husband that given the wife’s concerns, a different valuer, namely Imperial Valuations ought be appointed so as to ensure that there could be no complaint raised by the wife with respect to the identity of the appointed valuer. In light of the wife’s concerns, I consider that to be a sensible concession by the husband. I note that the orders made by the Senior Judicial Registrar addressed that issue.
The wife, in the course of her submissions, raised no concerns as to the identity of the valuer nominated in the December 2025 orders the subject of review.
It was also submitted on behalf of the husband that the wife’s complaints as to “balance and proportionality” are complaints focusing on matters that have otherwise been addressed by the trial judge. It was submitted that the Application for Review was either an application designed to frustrate the orderly preparation for trial or that it was a “tit-for-tat” style of application.
It was submitted that such application was not a proper basis for the disposal of the valuation orders. In circumstances where there has been no testing of evidence, I am not in a position to make any findings in relation to those matters and I will refrain from doing so. However, the focus of the wife on the need for “proportionality”, notwithstanding her apparent concessions as to the need for an updated valuation, was a concerning feature of the manner in which she conducted this case.
I am satisfied that, in circumstances where the final hearing of the parties’ competing s 79 applications is listed in a period of a little more than two months, and where one of the assets the subject of those applications, being the property at C Street, Suburb B, has not been valued by a jointly appointed single expert valuer since June 2024, it is necessary and appropriate that there be an updated valuation of that property.
It would not be appropriate for the appointment of that valuer to await the commencement of trial. To enable the parties to properly prepare their cases, the valuations must be undertaken prior to trial. The orders that have been made by Senior Judicial Registrar Sudholz enable that process to occur. Accordingly, I am not satisfied that there is any basis for a discharge of orders 2 and 3 of the orders of the Senior Judicial Registrar made on 4 December 2025.
Accordingly, I am satisfied that the wife’s Application for Review ought be dismissed and that the orders of the Senior Judicial Registrar stand.
NOTE: These reasons were delivered ex tempore and have been corrected from the transcript. Topic headings have been inserted and grammatical errors have been corrected. In addition, amendments have been made to make the rally delivered reasons clear and easy to read.
